children.name: Clupea
children.description: Atlantic and Pacific herrings — the most commercially important fish in the world by total catch. Baltic herring (strömming) and Atlantic herring (sill) are central to Swedish food culture in a way that transcends cuisine: fermented Baltic herring (surströmming) is a legally protected Swedish cultural delicacy whose annual opening season triggers international headlines. Herring move in vast, coordinated shoals of millions of fish, a defensive anti-predator strategy that creates one of the most spectacular wildlife phenomena in the sea — the 'bait balls' that attract gannets, whales, and tuna simultaneously. Their fat content varies dramatically through the year, peaking in autumn when they are harvested for pickling and fermentation.

children.name: Clupea harengus
children.description: The Baltic subspecies (C. h. membras) is smaller and less fatty than the North Sea herring — this is the strömming of Swedish cuisine, sold fermented as surströmming or pickled as inlagd sill. One of the most important prey fish for cod, seals, and seabirds in the North and Baltic Seas.

children.name: Clupea pallasii
children.description: The Pacific counterpart of the Atlantic herring and equally commercially important. Herring roe (kazunoko) is a prized New Year food in Japan. Spawning events in British Columbia turn whole bays bright green with eggs and attract eagles, sea lions, and grey whales.

children.name: Clupea bentincki
children.description: A small herring endemic to the cool, upwelling-rich waters off Chile and Peru. A major forage fish of the Humboldt Current and an important component of Chile's pelagic fishery.

children.name: Clupea microps
children.description: A herring of the northeast Atlantic, sometimes treated as a subspecies of the Atlantic herring. Distinguished by its slightly smaller eye and more slender body.

children.name: Clupea crassicornis
children.description: A herring of the northern Pacific coasts of Canada and Alaska, characterised by more prominent ridges on the operculum.

children.name: Clupea manulensis
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Clupea suworowi
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Clupea delicatus
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea asper
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea dactyloides
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea confinis
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea centrimaculatus
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea sinensis
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea carbonarius
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea discedens
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea decussatus
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea cyaneus
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea duplex
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea caniceps
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea luridus
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea nitidus
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea devius
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea decoratus
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea cuticulus
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Clupea chapadensis
children.description: A species of Clupea found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Sprattus
children.description: Sprats — small, herring-like clupeids of northern European and southern hemisphere seas. The Baltic sprat (skarpsill) forms one of the largest fish populations in the Baltic Sea and is the primary food fish for Baltic cod, salmon, and sea trout. Sprats are also heavily fished for human consumption, smoked and canned in Scandinavia (ansjovis — despite the name, these are sprats, not anchovies). The sprat's relationship with the Baltic cod is a classic predator-prey cycle: when cod collapsed in the 1980s–90s due to overfishing, sprat populations exploded, crashing the zooplankton communities that their larvae depended on.

children.name: Sprattus sprattus
children.description: The Baltic subspecies (S. s. balticus) is the most abundant fish in the Baltic Sea by biomass. Critical prey for cod, salmon, and seabirds. Canned smoked sprats are sold as 'ansjovis' across Scandinavia and are a classic Swedish pizza topping.

children.name: Sprattus antipodum
children.description: A small schooling sprat found in coastal and shelf waters around New Zealand and southern Australia. Reaches about 11 cm; an important prey item for snapper, trevally, and seabirds in the region.

children.name: Sprattus fuegensis
children.description: A cold-water sprat of the Magellan Strait and Tierra del Fuego region, reaching roughly 13 cm. Forms dense shoals in sub-Antarctic channels and is a primary prey item for Magellanic penguins and southern fur seals.

children.name: Sprattus muelleri
children.description: Inhabits cool shelf waters around the Falkland Islands and Patagonian Shelf. A small but ecologically important schooling clupeid serving as forage for rockhopper penguins and black-browed albatrosses.

children.name: Sprattus novaehollandiae
children.description: Found in southern Australian and Tasmanian coastal waters, typically in schools near the surface over sandy or rocky substrates. Commercially harvested for canning and for use as live bait in tuna and kingfish fisheries.

children.name: Sprattus fuelegens
children.description: A sprat of the cold-temperate waters around the Falkland Islands and southern Patagonia. A critical prey species for penguins, cormorants, and sea lions in the South Atlantic.

children.name: Sprattus salpus
children.description: A sprat of the Benguela Current off Namibia and South Africa. Its name reflects its association with salp blooms in the nutrient-rich waters of the Benguela upwelling system.

children.name: Sprattus holodon
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Sprattus salpoides
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa
children.description: Shads — large, anadromous clupeids that migrate from the sea into rivers to spawn, similar in life history to salmon. Allis shad and twaite shad once ran up European rivers in extraordinary numbers; like salmon, they were blocked by weirs and dams and decimated by river pollution through the 19th and 20th centuries. The twaite shad (staksill in Swedish) migrates into rivers including the Rhine and is the subject of active restoration programs in Britain and Germany. American shad (Alosa sapidissima) was once the most important food fish on the east coast of North America — 'sapidissima' means 'most delicious'.

children.name: Alosa fallax
children.description: Vulnerable in Europe; makes spring spawning migrations into major rivers. The Wye and Severn rivers in Britain hold the most important UK populations. Can produce audible sounds by vibrating its swimbladder during courtship. Very sensitive to river barriers.

children.name: Alosa alosa
children.description: The largest European shad, anadromous, migrating from the Atlantic into rivers to spawn. Once abundant in the Rhine, Loire and Garonne but now Vulnerable due to dams and pollution; the Gironde-Garonne-Dordogne system holds a key remaining population.

children.name: Alosa sapidissima
children.description: Anadromous shad of the North American Atlantic coast; the specific epithet means 'most delicious'. Once the most economically important food fish on the US east coast, it was introduced to the Pacific coast in the 1870s where it became established.

children.name: Alosa pseudoharengus
children.description: Small anadromous shad of eastern North America that has formed landlocked populations in the Great Lakes, where it became invasive and disrupted native food webs. A key forage fish historically used as fertilizer and bait.

children.name: Alosa aestivalis
children.description: Anadromous river herring of the US Atlantic coast, similar to the alewife but spawning later in warmer water. Populations have declined sharply from overfishing and dams, prompting fishing moratoriums in several states.

children.name: Alosa immaculata
children.description: Anadromous shad of the Black Sea and Sea of Azov that migrates up the Danube to spawn. An important commercial fish in the lower Danube; its long-distance river migrations are threatened by dams.

children.name: Alosa tanaica
children.description: A small anadromous shad of the Black Sea and Sea of Azov basins that ascends rivers such as the Don and Dnieper to spawn. Locally important to fisheries and sensitive to river damming.

children.name: Alosa mediocris
children.description: A medium-sized anadromous shad of the US Atlantic coast from Maine to Florida, reaching about 60 cm. Unlike most shads it feeds actively on fish and squid; it is caught by sport anglers for its spirited runs and aerial leaps.

children.name: Alosa chrysochloris
children.description: The only fully freshwater shad of the Mississippi basin, ranging from the Great Lakes to the Gulf Coast rivers. Reaches 45 cm and feeds voraciously on small fish; a popular sport fish known for spectacular surface chases of prey.

children.name: Alosa ohiensis
children.description: A large river shad found in the Ohio and Mississippi river systems, reaching around 50 cm. Considered a landlocked derivative of the hickory shad; it is a swift, predatory clupeid and a prized catch among river anglers.

children.name: Alosa caspia
children.description: An anadromous shad of the Caspian Sea that ascends the Volga and Ural rivers to spawn, historically one of the most commercially important fishes in the basin. Populations have been severely reduced by dam construction, pollution and overfishing in the Soviet and post-Soviet eras.

children.name: Alosa kessleri
children.description: The largest Caspian shad, reaching over 50 cm, with a historically significant commercial fishery on the Volga and Caspian Sea. Like other Caspian shads it has declined dramatically following the damming of the Volga and the collapse of the Caspian fisheries.

children.name: Alosa alabamae
children.description: An anadromous shad of Gulf Coast river drainages from the Suwannee to the Mississippi, once abundant in the Alabama, Coosa and Cahaba rivers. Now critically threatened by dam blockages and reduced to remnant populations; one of the most imperilled clupeids in North America.

children.name: Alosa braschnikowi
children.description: A semi-anadromous shad endemic to the Caspian Sea, known from several subspecies occupying different parts of the basin. One of the rare Caspian endemics; population status is poorly monitored but faces ongoing threats from habitat modification.

children.name: Alosa maeotica
children.description: A small anadromous shad of the Black Sea and Sea of Azov, closely related to Alosa pontica but occupying different spawning rivers. Commercially fished in Turkey, Ukraine and Romania, though populations are sensitive to pollution and river regulation.

children.name: Alosa sphaerocephala
children.description: A landlocked or semi-anadromous shad of the lower Danube delta and associated Black Sea tributaries, distinct from Alosa immaculata by its rounded head profile. A range-restricted species of conservation concern; threatened by water extraction and eutrophication of its spawning habitats.

children.name: Alosa ohioensis
children.description: A shad of the Ohio River basin. Its populations collapsed after dam construction blocked spawning migrations in the early 20th century.

children.name: Alosa matsubarai
children.description: A Japanese shad of the Pacific coast, from Hokkaido to Kyushu. Its roe is considered a delicacy in Japanese cuisine, where it is known as 'kado-iko'.

children.name: Alosa agone
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa bulgarica
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa curensis
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa killarnensis
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa macedonica
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa macotica
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa saposchnikowii
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa suvorowi
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa vistonica
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa volgensis
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Alosa bicornis
children.description: A species of Alosa found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Alosa sinensis
children.description: A species of Alosa found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Alosa ellipsoides
children.description: A species of Alosa found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.description: A species of Alosa found across Europe, Asia and Africa.

children.name: Sardina
children.description: The European pilchard (sardine) — a single-species genus of the Mediterranean and eastern Atlantic. Mature fish are sold as pilchards, young fish as sardines. One of the most heavily fished species in Europe and a cornerstone of the Portuguese, Spanish and Mediterranean canning industries.

children.name: Sardina pilchardus
children.description: The classic canned sardine of the eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean. Grilled sardines are an icon of Portuguese summer festivals. A vital forage fish whose populations fluctuate with ocean temperature and upwelling.

children.name: Sardina pilchardus senegalensis
children.description: A subspecies of the European sardine found along the West African coast from Morocco to Senegal. It supports an important fishery in the Canary Current ecosystem.

children.name: Sardinops
children.description: The pilchards or 'true sardines' of the Pacific and Indian Oceans, often treated as a single circumglobal species with regional populations. The South African sardine run is one of the greatest marine migrations on Earth, with billions of fish followed by dolphins, sharks, gannets and whales.

children.name: Sardinops sagax
children.description: A circumglobal pilchard of cool, productive waters off South America, southern Africa, Australia, Japan and California. Its enormous, climate-driven boom-and-bust cycles drive entire fisheries; the South African 'sardine run' is a world-famous wildlife spectacle.

children.name: Sardinops caeruleus
children.description: The eastern Pacific stock of S. sagax found from British Columbia to Baja California. Subject to some of the most intensive fishery management measures in the world, with strict quotas that shut down entirely when biomass drops below 150,000 tonnes.

children.name: Sardinops melanostictus
children.description: The northwestern Pacific sardine, a major fishery species for Japan, Korea, and Russia. Its stocks undergo dramatic fluctuations, with documented boom-and-bust cycles extending back centuries in Japanese historical records.

children.name: Sardinops neopilchardus
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Sardinops ocellatus
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Sardinella
children.description: Sardinellas — a large genus of small, fast-growing tropical and subtropical clupeids. They dominate artisanal and commercial fisheries across West Africa, the Indian Ocean and Southeast Asia, providing critical protein and income for coastal communities.

children.name: Sardinella aurita
children.description: A widespread warm-water sardinella of both sides of the Atlantic and the Mediterranean. The most important small pelagic fishery off West Africa, where it is critical to food security; subject to growing concern over overfishing and warming seas.

children.name: Sardinella longiceps
children.description: One of the most important fishes of the Indian Ocean, supporting a massive fishery off India's southwest coast. Its abundance swings dramatically with the monsoon-driven upwelling, making catches highly variable year to year.

children.name: Sardinella gibbosa
children.description: A small Indo-West Pacific sardinella marked by a faint golden lateral stripe. One of the most commonly landed sardines across South and Southeast Asia, eaten fresh, dried and canned.

children.name: Sardinella lemuru
children.description: The dominant small pelagic fish of the Bali Strait between Bali and Java, supporting one of the most productive fisheries in Southeast Asia. Packed and canned in large quantities; its boom-and-bust cycles are linked to Java Sea upwelling patterns and climate oscillations.

children.name: Sardinella albella
children.description: A pale, silvery sardinella of the Indo-West Pacific coastal and estuarine waters from the Persian Gulf to the Philippines. Reaches about 15 cm; an important food fish caught mainly in artisanal gill-nets and beach seines.

children.name: Sardinella maderensis
children.description: The dominant small pelagic fish of the Gulf of Guinea and a critical food security species in West Africa, where it is smoked, dried and sold widely as 'banda'. Reaches 30 cm and is taken in huge quantities by purse seines off Senegal, Ghana and Nigeria.

children.name: Sardinella fimbriata
children.description: A widespread Indo-Pacific sardinella named for the fringed edges of its scales, distinguishable by its pronounced golden lateral stripe. Reaches about 14 cm; targeted by small-scale fisheries from India to the Philippines.

children.name: Sardinella melanura
children.description: Named for its distinctive black-tipped caudal fin, this sardinella inhabits shallow coastal and estuarine waters across the Indo-West Pacific. Reaches about 14 cm and is commonly caught by artisanal fishers.

children.name: Sardinella sindensis
children.description: A small sardinella of the northern Arabian Sea and Persian Gulf, particularly important in the fisheries of Pakistan (Sindh) and India. Often caught in large numbers by beach seines and consumed fresh or dried locally.

children.name: Sardinella tawilis
children.description: The only known purely freshwater sardinella in the world, found exclusively in Lake Taal (Taal Lake) in the Philippines. Reaching about 14 cm, it is an iconic local food fish called 'tawilis' and is listed as Vulnerable due to its extremely restricted range.

children.name: Sardinella perforata
children.description: Named for its distinctively pitted (perforated) scale surfaces, this species inhabits shallow tropical coastal waters from India to Indonesia. A smaller sardinella reaching about 17 cm, commonly taken in ring nets and cast nets.

children.name: Sardinella zunasi
children.description: An inshore sardinella of the Yellow Sea, East China Sea, and Sea of Japan, also called the Korean sardinella. Reaches about 18 cm; an important baitfish and food fish in Chinese and Korean coastal fisheries.

children.name: Sardinella marquesensis
children.description: A sardinella known only from the Marquesas Islands of French Polynesia, one of the most geographically restricted clupeids in the Pacific. A small, inshore schooling species of reef-adjacent waters; data-deficient and potentially affected by climate-driven changes to Pacific island fisheries.

children.name: Sardinella richardsoni
children.description: A sardinella of the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden extending into the northern Indian Ocean, reaching about 17 cm. Taken in artisanal fisheries and valued locally as a food fish; named after the naturalist John Richardson.

children.name: Brevoortia
children.description: Menhaden — deep-bodied, oily clupeids of the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of the Americas. Rarely eaten directly, they are caught in huge volumes for fish meal, oil and omega-3 supplements, and are ecologically vital as filter feeders and as prey for striped bass and ospreys.

children.name: Brevoortia tyrannus
children.description: An extremely abundant filter-feeding clupeid of the US Atlantic coast, central to the largest fishery by volume on the east coast. Called 'the most important fish in the sea' for its role linking plankton to predators like striped bass, tuna and ospreys.

children.name: Brevoortia patronus
children.description: The dominant menhaden of the Gulf of Mexico and basis of one of the largest fisheries in the USA by weight. Harvested almost entirely for fish meal and fish oil reduction.

children.name: Brevoortia smithi
children.description: A smaller menhaden of the US Atlantic coast from North Carolina to Florida, distinguished by yellowish pectoral fins. Less commercially important than the Atlantic menhaden but ecologically significant in nearshore estuarine food webs.

children.name: Brevoortia gunteri
children.description: A coastal menhaden of the Gulf of Mexico from Louisiana to Mexico with characteristically fine, intricate scale patterning. Reaches about 30 cm; a forage fish for coastal predators and an underutilised bycatch in Gulf menhaden fisheries.

children.name: Brevoortia aurea
children.description: A South American menhaden of the coastal and estuarine waters of Argentina, Uruguay and southern Brazil, reaching about 35 cm. The basis of a large reduction fishery in the Río de la Plata estuary producing fish meal and oil.

children.name: Brevoortia pecca
children.description: A small menhaden of the southeastern US Atlantic coast and Gulf coast. Its finer scales and smaller maximum size distinguish it from B. tyrannus.

children.name: Brevoortia pectinata
children.description: A silvery schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters. It is an important forage species for larger predators.

children.name: Tenualosa
children.description: Tropical shads of South and Southeast Asia, including the hilsa — an anadromous fish of immense cultural and economic value in Bangladesh and eastern India, where it is the national fish of Bangladesh.

children.name: Tenualosa ilisha
children.description: The national fish of Bangladesh and the most economically important single fish species of the country. Anadromous, migrating from the Bay of Bengal up the Ganges-Brahmaputra and other rivers; prized for its rich, oily flesh and central to Bengali cuisine and identity.

children.name: Tenualosa toli
children.description: A large anadromous shad of the Indo-West Pacific from the Persian Gulf to Indonesia, reaching 50 cm and over 1 kg. Highly regarded as food for its rich, oily flesh; commercially fished in Bangladesh, Myanmar and Sumatra.

children.name: Tenualosa macrura
children.description: A riverine shad named for its proportionally long caudal fin, found in the coastal rivers and estuaries of Borneo and the Malay Peninsula. Locally important to artisanal fisheries and susceptible to habitat loss from river development.

children.name: Tenualosa reevesii
children.description: An anadromous shad that ascends the Pearl River, Yangtze and other major rivers of China, reaching about 40 cm. A prized food fish of southern China, whose upstream migrations are now severely curtailed by dam construction.

children.name: Tenualosa thibaudeaui
children.description: A semi-anadromous shad of the Mekong River system in Laos and Cambodia, one of the few clupeids endemic to the Lower Mekong. An important food and cultural fish in Laos, listed as Vulnerable given its restricted range and sensitivity to Mekong dams.

children.name: Tenualosa thibaudeawi
children.description: A freshwater shad of the Mekong River basin in Laos and Cambodia. Unlike most Tenualosa, it is entirely freshwater.

children.name: Dorosoma
children.description: Gizzard shads of North and Central America — freshwater and estuarine clupeids named for their muscular, gizzard-like stomach used to grind detritus and plankton. They are crucial forage fish in reservoirs and rivers, often dominating fish biomass.

children.name: Dorosoma cepedianum
children.description: A widespread filter-feeding shad of North American rivers, lakes and reservoirs. A vital forage fish for bass, walleye and catfish; its winterkills and population booms strongly shape freshwater food webs.

children.name: Dorosoma petenense
children.description: A small gizzard shad of warm rivers, reservoirs and lakes from the Mississippi basin to Guatemala and Belize, widely introduced as a forage fish throughout the southern USA. One of the most important forage fishes for largemouth bass and striped bass in impounded waters.

children.name: Dorosoma anale
children.description: A small gizzard shad of Pacific-slope river drainages in Mexico from Jalisco to Chiapas, tolerating both fresh and brackish water. Locally abundant in lagoons and coastal rivers; an important prey species for coastal predators.

children.name: Dorosoma chavesi
children.description: A lesser-known gizzard shad of Central American river systems in Guatemala and southern Mexico. Reaches about 20 cm; largely unstudied biologically but fills a key herbivorous filter-feeding role in its river ecosystems.

children.name: Dorosoma mexicanum
children.description: An endemic gizzard shad of the Grijalva and Usumacinta river drainages in southern Mexico and Guatemala. One of the smaller dorosomines, restricted to clear, warm lowland rivers and considered Near Threatened due to watershed development.

children.name: Dorosoma smithi
children.description: A gizzard shad of the Pacific coast of Central America, from Mexico to Nicaragua.

children.name: Opisthonema
children.description: Thread herrings of the Americas, named for the long, trailing last ray of the dorsal fin. They are important coastal pelagic fish in the eastern Pacific and western Atlantic, fished for canning, bait and reduction to fish meal.

children.name: Opisthonema oglinum
children.description: A schooling coastal clupeid of the western Atlantic from the USA to Brazil, recognizable by its elongated last dorsal-fin ray. Widely used as bait and increasingly harvested for fish meal.

children.name: Opisthonema libertate
children.description: A schooling coastal clupeid of the eastern Pacific from Baja California to Ecuador, forming large nearshore schools. Supports major reduction fisheries in Mexico and is a key prey item for yellowfin tuna, sea lions and seabirds.

children.name: Opisthonema medirastre
children.description: A medium-sized thread herring of the eastern Pacific coast from Mexico to Peru, closely related to O. libertate. Caught in purse seines and used for reduction and canning; frequently co-occurs with anchovies in upwelling zones.

children.name: Opisthonema bulleri
children.description: A slender thread herring endemic to the Gulf of California and adjacent Mexican Pacific coast. Reaches about 25 cm; less commercially important than related species but locally significant in artisanal bait fisheries.

children.name: Ethmalosa
children.description: The bonga shad — a single-species genus of estuarine West African clupeid that tolerates a wide range of salinity. It is one of the most important fishes for West African artisanal fisheries, eaten smoked and dried.

children.name: Ethmalosa fimbriata
children.description: An estuarine filter-feeding clupeid ranging along West Africa from Mauritania to Angola. A staple of West African artisanal fisheries, traditionally smoked; populations are under pressure from intensive fishing and habitat change.

children.name: Harengula
children.description: Scaled sardines or pilchards of the warm western Atlantic. Small, silvery, schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters, they are among the most important baitfish for sport and commercial fishing in the Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico.

children.name: Harengula jaguana
children.description: An abundant inshore clupeid of the western Atlantic from the USA to Brazil, common over seagrass and sandy bottoms. A premier live bait for tarpon, snook and other gamefish.

children.name: Harengula clupeola
children.description: A small schooling clupeid of the Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico coasts, identifiable by a reddish tinge on the operculum. Reaches about 15 cm; important as baitfish and as a prey item for jacks, barracuda and seabirds.

children.name: Harengula humeralis
children.description: A silvery inshore clupeid of the Caribbean Sea with a distinctive reddish humeral spot behind the gill cover. Typically found in shallow bays and estuaries; important in artisanal fisheries and as live bait in the Caribbean.

children.name: Harengula thrissina
children.description: A small scaled sardine of the eastern Pacific coast from the Gulf of California to Ecuador. Forms inshore schools in bays and estuaries; heavily used as live bait in Pacific coast sport fisheries and harvested for local consumption.

children.name: Hilsa
children.description: Kelee shads of the Indo-Pacific — coastal and estuarine clupeids related to the hilsa. Distinguished by dark spots behind the gill cover; they support local fisheries across the Indian Ocean and western Pacific.

children.name: Hilsa kelee
children.description: A coastal shad of the Indo-West Pacific, identified by a row of dark blotches along the upper flank. Caught throughout its range in artisanal fisheries and valued as food.

children.name: Hilsa keelee
children.description: A large shad of the coastal waters of India and Bangladesh, closely related to the more famous tenualosa ilisha but distinguished by body shape and gill raker counts.

children.name: Hilsa toli
children.description: A shad of estuaries and rivers along the Indian subcontinent's west coast. Its flesh is prized in the coastal cuisines of Gujarat and Karnataka.

children.name: Konosirus
children.description: Dotted gizzard shads — a monotypic genus of the East Asian coast, resembling both the gizzard shads (Dorosoma) and thread herrings in morphology. The punctatus is culturally significant in Japan and China as a food fish and spawning-run spectacle.

children.name: Konosirus punctatus
children.description: A coastal clupeid of the East China Sea, Yellow Sea and Japanese coastal waters, distinguished by a trailing dorsal-fin filament and rows of dark dots on the flanks. An important commercial species in China and Japan eaten grilled, salted and fermented.

children.name: Etrumeus
children.description: Round herrings — slender, cylindrical clupeids of warm and temperate seas worldwide, distinguished from typical herrings by their rounded belly lacking sharp scutes. They form large offshore schools and support purse-seine fisheries.

children.name: Etrumeus sadina
children.description: A slender, round-bodied herring of the western Atlantic, named for its conspicuous red eye. Forms dense offshore schools and is harvested for bait and food.

children.name: Etrumeus teres
children.description: A widespread Indo-Pacific round herring forming large offshore schools. Targeted by purse-seine fisheries and an important forage fish for tuna and other predators.

children.name: Nematalosa
children.description: Gizzard shads of the Indo-Pacific, with a muscular gizzard-like stomach and a trailing last dorsal ray. They inhabit coastal, estuarine and freshwater habitats and are caught locally for food.

children.name: Nematalosa nasus
children.description: A bottom-feeding gizzard shad widespread across the Indo-West Pacific, tolerant of brackish and fresh water. Common in estuaries and supports artisanal fisheries throughout southern Asia.

children.name: Nematalosa come
children.description: A small gizzard shad of northern Australian estuaries and coastal rivers, tolerant of a wide salinity range from fresh to full marine. Feeds by filtering detritus with its gizzard-like stomach and serves as prey for barramundi and larger coastal predators.

children.name: Escualosa
children.description: Whitefin or river sardines — small, delicate silvery clupeids of Indo-Pacific coasts and rivers, often forming dense inshore schools that are dip-netted for food and bait.

children.name: Escualosa thoracata
children.description: A tiny silvery clupeid of Indo-Pacific coastal and estuarine waters, often appearing in huge inshore shoals. Caught in large numbers for drying and as a delicacy when fresh.

children.name: Kowala
children.description: The slender white sardine — a single-species Indo-Pacific genus of small coastal clupeid, distinguished by serrated belly scutes and a slim body.

children.name: Kowala coval
children.description: A small schooling clupeid of Indo-West Pacific coastal waters with strongly keeled belly scutes. Caught in coastal seines and consumed fresh or dried across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Amblygaster
children.description: Smoothbelly sardinellas of the Indo-Pacific, similar to Sardinella but lacking sharp belly scutes. They form large offshore schools and are important to tropical small-pelagic fisheries.

children.name: Amblygaster sirm
children.description: A warm-water sardinella of the Indo-West Pacific marked by a row of golden spots along the flank. Forms large schools and is a major target of tropical purse-seine and ring-net fisheries.

children.name: Amblygaster leiogaster
children.description: An Indo-Pacific smoothbelly sardinella of shallow coastal waters from the Red Sea and East Africa to Indonesia and northern Australia. Reaches about 17 cm; an important artisanal catch, dried and consumed widely across Southeast Asia.

children.name: Gudusia
children.description: Freshwater river shads of South Asia, inhabiting the Ganges and other large river systems and floodplain lakes. They are important food fish in inland fisheries of the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Gudusia chapra
children.description: A small freshwater clupeid of the rivers, canals and floodplain lakes of South Asia, abundant in the Ganges-Brahmaputra basin. A common, inexpensive food fish in inland fisheries of India and Bangladesh.

children.name: Dussumieria
children.description: Rainbow sardines — slender, streamlined clupeids of tropical Indo-Pacific coastal waters, related to the round herrings. They form large surface schools and are caught in cast nets and ring nets for fresh consumption and drying.

children.name: Dussumieria acuta
children.description: A slender, iridescent coastal sardine of the Indo-West Pacific from India to the Philippines, reaching about 15 cm. Forms dense surface schools in coastal and estuarine waters; an important small-scale food fish across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Dussumieria elopsoides
children.description: A more elongate species of rainbow sardine found along tropical coasts from India to Melanesia. Superficially resembles a small ladyfish (Elops) in body shape; captured in coastal seine and ring-net fisheries throughout its range.

children.name: Pellona
children.description: Pellona herrings — large, deep-bodied clupeids of South American and West African rivers and estuaries, notable for their large, prominent scales and strongly compressed bodies. They are important food fish in Amazonian and Orinoco basin fisheries.

children.name: Pellona castelnaeana
children.description: A large riverine clupeid of the Amazon and Orinoco basins, reaching 55 cm, with conspicuously large silvery scales. An important commercial fish throughout the Amazon basin eaten fresh and salted; migratory within river systems.

children.name: Pellona flavipinnis
children.description: A large deep-bodied clupeid of South American Atlantic coastal rivers from the Orinoco to the Río de la Plata, reaching about 50 cm and notable for its yellowish fins. Commercially important in Brazil and Argentina; enters freshwater for reproduction.

children.name: Ilisha
children.description: Ilisha herrings — deep-bodied, strongly compressed clupeids of tropical Asian and African coastal and estuarine waters. They resemble shads in body shape and are important commercial food fish throughout their range.

children.name: Ilisha elongata
children.description: A large, deep-bodied clupeid of East and Southeast Asian coasts from Japan and China to Indonesia, reaching up to 50 cm. An important commercial fish marketed fresh and dried; enters estuaries and lower rivers.

children.name: Ilisha africana
children.description: A deep-bodied coastal and estuarine clupeid of West African waters from Senegal to Angola, reaching about 35 cm. An important artisanal food fish widely sold fresh and smoked in coastal markets from Ghana to Cameroon.

children.name: Pomolobus
children.description: Small anadromous herrings of North America that form a critical forage base for coastal and freshwater food webs.

children.name: Pomolobus chrysochloris
children.description: A fast-swimming shad of the Mississippi River basin and Gulf Coast. Its habit of leaping out of the water earned it the name 'skipjack'. An important forage fish for larger predators.
